alignments.preprint_claim_text: DEFORM-Net is experimentally unsupervised and does not require experimental ground truth. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DEFORM-Net is experimentally unsupervised and relies on displacement simulation based on a random fractal Perlin-noise process. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds methodological detail (Perlin-noise simulation) clarifying how unsupervised behavior is achieved and lowers stated certainty from high to medium, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DEFORM-Net has been demonstrated on real biological videos of beating neonatal mouse cardiomyocytes and pulsed contractions in Drosophila pupae. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DEFORM-Net has been demonstrated on real biological videos of beating neonatal mouse cardiomyocytes and pulsed contractions in Drosophila pupae. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Demonstration examples match exactly between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DEFORM-Net is provided as open source for rapid evaluation in the ImageJ/FIJI platform. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DEFORM-Net is provided as open source, including inference in the ImageJ/FIJI platform. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Publication clarifies that open-source release includes inference support in ImageJ/FIJI and raises the stated certainty, so the claim is clarified and confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MATES is a deep-learning approach that accurately allocates multi-mapping reads to specific loci of transposable elements. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MATES is a deep-learning approach that accurately allocates multi-mapping reads to specific loci of transposable elements. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Core contribution is identically stated in preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MATES shows improved performance over existing methods when applied to diverse single-cell omics datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MATES shows improved performance over existing methods when applied to diverse single-cell omics datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Performance claim is unchanged between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MATES enhances the accuracy of transposable element quantification and aids in the identification of marker transposable elements for identified cell populations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: MATES enhances the accuracy of transposable element quantification and aids in the identification of marker transposable elements for identified cell populations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Result about improved quantification and marker identification is the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The development of MATES facilitates the exploration of single-cell heterogeneity and gene regulation through the lens of transposable elements. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The development of MATES facilitates the exploration of single-cell heterogeneity and gene regulation through the lens of transposable elements. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion about enabling downstream biological exploration is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The developed convolutional neural networks (CNNs) can accurately detect and measure butterfly spots and eyespots on digital images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Distinct convolutional neural networks (CNNs) were trained for the detection and measurement of butterfly spots and eyespots on digital images of butterfly wings.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same result—that CNNs were trained to detect and measure spots/eyespots on images—without substantive change in meaning or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNNs were able to distinguish marginal eyespots from spots and differentiate these patterns from less symmetrical patches of color.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNNs were able to identify and distinguish marginal eyespots from spots and from less symmetrical patches of color.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same capability to discriminate eyespot types and other patches with no substantive alteration to the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Measurements of an eyespot's central area and surrounding rings using the CNNs were highly accurate.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Measurements of an eyespot's central area and surrounding rings obtained from the CNNs were comparable to manual measurements.
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes 'highly accurate' as being 'comparable to manual measurements', which clarifies the evaluation reference and slightly tempers the absolute accuracy claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The CNNs improve eyespot/spot detection and measurements compared to previous methods by eliminating the need for mathematical definitions of features.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The CNNs offer improvements in eyespot/spot detection and measurements because it is not necessary to mathematically define the feature of interest.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes the same methodological/conceptual claim about improvements due to removing the need for explicit mathematical feature definitions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High throughput computing was performed to calculate the SCM scores for 6596 nonredundant antibody variable regions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: High throughput computing was performed to calculate the SCM scores for 6596 nonredundant antibody variable regions.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this methodological/result statement verbatim, with no change in meaning or emphasis.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The linear correlation coefficient of the DeepSCM and SCM scores achieved 0.9 on the test set (N=1320).
alignments.pub_claim_text: The linear correlation coefficient of the DeepSCM and SCM scores achieved 0.9 on the test set (N = 1320).
alignments.rationale: The reported correlation and test-set size are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DeepSCM model was applied to screen the viscosity of 38 therapeutic antibodies that SCM correctly classified and resulted in only one misclassification.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DeepSCM model was applied to screen the viscosity of 38 therapeutic antibodies that SCM correctly classified and resulted in only one misclassification.
alignments.rationale: The application and outcome (one misclassification) are presented identically in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The DeepSCM model will facilitate high concentration antibody viscosity screening.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The DeepSCM model will facilitate high concentration antibody viscosity screening.
alignments.rationale: The forward-looking conclusion about the model's utility is unchanged between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The hippocampus is capable of extracting regularities across experiences, which is central to category learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The hippocampus is capable of extracting regularities across experiences, which is central to category learning.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same conclusion about the hippocampus extracting regularities for category learning without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The trisynaptic pathway within the hippocampus is critical for remembering exemplar-specific information.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The trisynaptic pathway within the hippocampus is critical for remembering exemplar-specific information.
alignments.rationale: The role attributed to the trisynaptic pathway is consistently described in both preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The monosynaptic pathway from entorhinal cortex to CA1 specializes in detecting the regularities that define category structure across exemplars.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The monosynaptic pathway from entorhinal cortex to CA1 specializes in detecting the regularities that define category structure across exemplars.
alignments.rationale: The textual claim is the same, but the publication reports a lower certainty level (medium vs high), indicating a tempered confidence in this specific pathway specialization.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The simulations provide an account of how the hippocampus and its constituent pathways support novel category learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The simulations provide an account of how the hippocampus and its constituent pathways support novel category learning.
alignments.rationale: The claim content is the same, but the publication expresses higher certainty (high vs medium), representing an amplification of confidence in the simulations' explanatory value.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) have achieved unequaled performance for numerous problems in many areas of Science, Business, Public Policy, and more.
alignments.rationale: This broad, declarative claim about ANNs' unmatched performance does not appear in the publication; it was omitted from the final paper.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EpyNN is an educational python resource designed to help non-experts understand key concepts and practical implementation of scalable ANN architectures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EpyNN is an educational Python resource designed to help non-experts understand key concepts and practical implementation of scalable Artificial Neural Network architectures.
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the same contribution statement about EpyNN being an educational Python resource for non-experts.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EpyNN includes an educational Application Programming Interface (API), workflows from data preparation to ANN training, and a documentation website to facilitate learning.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EpyNN includes an educational Application Programming Interface (API) and workflows that guide users from data preparation to ANN training.
alignments.rationale: The publication splits the multi-part preprint claim: Q1 covers API and workflows while documentation details are given separately (see Q2), so the original combined claim is clarified across two publication claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: EpyNN provides a basis for python-fluent individuals who wish to learn, teach, or develop ANN architectures from scratch.
alignments.pub_claim_text: EpyNN provides foundational knowledge in Python for individuals interested in learning, teaching, or developing Artificial Neural Networks from scratch.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same concluding educational utility of EpyNN with equivalent meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method for identifying enoxacin-resistant Escherichia coli cells achieved an accuracy rate of 0.94.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method for identifying enoxacin-resistant Escherichia coli cells achieved an accuracy rate of 0.94.
alignments.rationale: The reported accuracy is identical in the publication and the preprint, indicating no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Morphological changes in enoxacin-resistant E. coli cells can be identified using a computational method without the use of antibiotics.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Morphological changes in enoxacin-resistant Escherichia coli cells can be identified without using antibiotics.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same conclusion that morphological changes can be detected without antibiotics; the computational-method context is implicit in the paper and not meaningfully altered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Four genes, including lpp, were strongly associated with the image features of enoxacin-resistant cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Four genes, including lpp, were strongly associated with the image features of enoxacin-resistant cells.
alignments.rationale: The association between the four genes (including lpp) and image features is preserved in the publication, though its label shifts to a concluding statement.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DisDock is a physics-driven deep learning method for predicting protein-metal docking. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DisDock is a deep learning method for predicting protein-metal docking. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution; wording difference ('physics-driven' omitted) does not change the claim's intent or scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DisDock combines the U-net architecture with self-attention modules to enhance model performance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: DisDock combines the U-net architecture with self-attention modules to enhance model performance. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Identical description of model architecture and intended effect, no change in scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms other existing methods in prediction accuracy for various types of metal ions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Experimental results demonstrate that our approach outperforms other existing methods in prediction accuracy for various types of metal ions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental result claim is carried over verbatim with no qualifiers changed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The HLM method can effectively distinguish different types of filaments in the presence of many contaminants and low signal-to-noise ratios. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method HLM (Helical classification with Language Model) can effectively distinguish different types of filaments in the presence of many contaminants and low signal-to-noise ratios. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same operational claim; publication only expands the acronym but preserves the claim content and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: HLM can isolate homogeneous subsets of particles from a publicly available dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: HLM can isolate homogeneous subsets of particles from a publicly available dataset. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical claim about isolating homogeneous subsets; no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of HLM resulted in the discovery of a previously unknown non-proteinaceous density around tau filaments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of HLM resulted in the discovery of a previously unreported filament variant with an extra density around the tau filaments. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the finding as a 'filament variant with an extra density' rather than asserting a 'non-proteinaceous' identity, changing the interpretation and narrowing the compositional assertion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The weakly supervised learning approach using bulk RNA sequencing predicts PDL1 expression from H&E slides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The weakly supervised learning approach using bulk RNA sequencing predicts PDL1 expression from H&E slides. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The core result is carried over exactly between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The MILTS method achieves a weighted average AUC of 0.83 on fresh-frozen specimens and 0.74 on formalin-fixed specimens for PDL1 expression prediction. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The method achieves a weighted average area under the curve of 0.83 on fresh-frozen and 0.74 on formalin-fixed specimens for 9 tumors with PDL1 as an established biomarker. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the AUC numbers but adds the context 'for 9 tumors with PDL1 as an established biomarker,' which narrows and clarifies the applicability.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach offers insights into histologies relevant to PDL1, validated by immunohistochemistry on 20 slides. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The approach offers insights into histologies relevant to PDL1, validated by IHC on 20 slides. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same validation claim; only abbreviation 'IHC' used in the publication, no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This demonstrates the potential of deep learning in identifying diverse histological patterns for molecular changes from H&E images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This demonstrates the potential of deep learning in identifying diverse histological patterns for molecular changes from H&E images. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The concluding statement is preserved verbatim between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We report a thermosensory chemical neuron based on DNA and enzymes that spikes with chemical activity when exposed to cold. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We report a thermosensory chemical neuron based on DNA and enzymes that spikes with chemical activity when exposed to cold. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The experimental report is carried forward verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This chemical neuron shares deep mathematical similarities with a toy model of a cold nociceptive neuron. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This chemical neuron shares deep mathematical similarities with a toy model of a cold nociceptive neuron. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The conceptual similarity claim is unchanged in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We experimentally demonstrate the robustness of this chemical neuron by encoding thermal messages into chemical waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We experimentally demonstrate the robustness of this chemical neuron by encoding thermal messages into chemical waveforms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Experimental robustness demonstration is stated identically.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This chemical neuron could pave the way for implementing in DNA the third generation of neural network models (spiking networks). (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This chemical neuron could pave the way for implementing the third generation of neural network models in DNA and opens the door for associative learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the 'pave the way' claim but expands it by explicitly linking to associative learning, broadening the suggested implications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The chemical neuron opens the door for associative learning. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This chemical neuron could pave the way for implementing the third generation of neural network models in DNA and opens the door for associative learning. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The associative-learning claim appears explicitly in the publication; its presentation is slightly stronger (combined with other claims), raising the effective confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: The chemical neuron follows a similar bifurcation route between rest and oscillations and avoids artefacts associated with canonical bifurcations. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This specific dynamical-bifurcation characterization is new in the publication and has no counterpart in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Lateral Accessory Lobe in the insect brain is involved in generating small scale search movements that enhance sensory sampling efficacy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The Lateral Accessory Lobe in the insect brain is involved in generating small scale search movements that enhance sensory sampling efficacy. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The functional conclusion about the Lateral Accessory Lobe is identical between versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Searching movements are initiated when the reliability of an essential navigational stimulus is low, while a targeted steering response is elicited when the stimulus reliability is high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Searching movements are initiated when the reliability of an essential navigational stimulus is low, while a targeted steering response is elicited when stimulus reliability is high. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The result about stimulus-reliability-dependent movement selection is preserved verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Spiking Neural Network models can generate a variety of adaptive movement patterns, predominantly zig-zagging movements observed in various insects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Spiking Neural Network models can generate a variety of adaptive movement patterns, predominantly zig-zagging movements observed in various insects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The modelling result about generating adaptive, predominantly zig-zagging patterns is unchanged.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The investigated network architecture is inherently well suited to generating adaptive movement patterns due to its robustness to noise and the large spread of network parameters leading to zig-zagging dynamics. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The investigated network architecture is inherently well-suited to generating adaptive movement patterns due to its robustness to noise and the large spread of network parameters leading to correct movement dynamics. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication changes 'zig-zagging dynamics' to the more general 'correct movement dynamics' and raises certainty to high, which revises the specificity and amplifies confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Multivariate Pattern Dependence (MVPD) is a powerful and flexible approach that trains and tests multivariate models of the interactions between brain regions using independent data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Multivariate pattern dependence (MVPD) is a powerful and flexible approach that trains and tests multivariate models of the interactions between brain regions using independent data. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: Text is essentially identical between preprint and publication, expressing the same contribution with the same level of certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Artificial neural networks outperform linear regression in modeling the interactions between brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Artificial neural networks outperform linear regression in modeling the interactions between brain regions. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is reproduced verbatim in the publication with unchanged result and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The best performing architecture for modeling interactions is region-dependent, with MVPD subdividing cortex into distinct, contiguous regions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The best performing architecture for modeling interactions is region-dependent, with MVPD subdividing cortex into distinct, contiguous regions. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Conclusion appears verbatim in the publication with the same scope and stated certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Deep convolutional neural networks can directly predict a variety of protein functions from an unaligned amino acid sequence. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: ProteInfer employs deep convolutional neural networks to directly predict a variety of protein functions from an unaligned amino acid sequence. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same capability but names the tool (ProteInfer), which clarifies implementation while preserving the original claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach provides precise predictions that complement traditional alignment-based methods. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This approach provides precise predictions which complement alignment-based methods. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same claim about complementarity and precision with equivalent certainty; classification and wording are effectively the same.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational efficiency of a single neural network allows for the development of novel and lightweight software interfaces for protein function prediction. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The computational efficiency of a single neural network permits novel and lightweight software interfaces for protein function prediction. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The claim content is the same but the publication expresses higher confidence (medium -> high), so the semantic content is unchanged while confidence is amplified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The models place full-length amino acid sequences into a generalised functional space, facilitating downstream analysis and interpretation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The models place full-length amino acid sequences into a generalised functional space, facilitating downstream analysis and interpretation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim is carried into the publication verbatim with the same scope and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 3D convolutional neural networks can learn the local fitness landscape of protein structure to reliably predict the wild-type amino acid. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: 3D convolutional neural networks can learn the local fitness landscape of protein structure to reliably predict the wild-type amino acid. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The same technical result is stated in both versions with matching certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Network confidence is a reliable measure of whether a given prediction of the wild type is likely to be correct. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Network confidence is a reliable measure of whether a given prediction of the wild type is likely to be correct. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the same claim about confidence as a reliability measure with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Predictions of consensus are less accurate and are primarily driven by whether or not the consensus matches the wild type. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Predictions of consensus are less accurate and are primarily driven by whether or not the consensus matches the wild type. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Claim about consensus predictions is reproduced without substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: High-confidence mis-predictions of the wild type may identify sites that are primed for mutation and likely targets for protein engineering. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: High-confidence mis-predictions of the wild type may identify sites that are primed for mutation and likely targets for protein engineering. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The speculative conclusion about high-confidence mis-predictions is unchanged in the publication with the same low certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Mask R-CNN can be used for automated brain extractions of piglet brains. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mask R-CNN can be used for automated brain extractions of piglet brains. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The core conclusion about Mask R-CNN applicability is preserved verbatim in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The approach was validated using Nested Cross-Validation on six sets of training/validation data drawn from 32 pigs. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The specific validation-detail claim (nested CV on six splits from 32 pigs) does not appear in the publication claims list and appears to have been omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Visual inspection of the extractions shows acceptable accuracy with Dice coefficients in the range of 0.95-0.97. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The accuracy of the brain extractions is acceptable, with Dice coefficients ranging from 0.95 to 0.97. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Dice coefficient results are reproduced directly in the publication with equivalent wording and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Hausdorff Distance values for the extractions are in the range of 4.1-8.3 voxels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Hausdorff Distance values for the brain extractions range from 4.1 to 8.3 voxels. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Hausdorff distance range is carried over unchanged into the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: R-CNNs provide a viable tool for skull stripping of piglet brains. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Mask R-CNN can be used for automated brain extractions of piglet brains. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: This preprint conclusion overlaps substantially with Q0; the publication retains the viability conclusion though expressed as the Mask R-CNN applicability claim.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Automated brain extractions improve the uniformity of the extractions compared to manual methods. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds an additional claim about improved uniformity relative to manual methods that was not present among the preprint claims.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Networks trained on generic object recognition perform poorly on face recognition and vice versa. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Networks trained on object recognition perform poorly on face recognition and vice versa. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The empirical result about cross-task performance is stated the same way in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Networks optimized for both tasks spontaneously segregate themselves into separate systems for faces and objects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Networks optimized for both tasks spontaneously segregate themselves into separate systems for faces and objects. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same result about spontaneous segregation with unchanged certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Generic visual features that suffice for object recognition are apparently suboptimal for face recognition and vice versa. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that the functional segregation of face recognition in the brain reflects a computational optimization for visual recognition tasks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication reframes the machine-level conclusion as a broader brain-level implication and expresses this more cautiously, so the claim is revised and its scope broadened while confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Functional segregation is revealed to varying degrees for other visual categories. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Functional segregation occurs to varying degrees for other visual categories, indicating a widespread tendency for optimization to lead to functional specialization. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the core observation but clarifies it by linking it to a broader tendency for optimization to produce specialization; scope is modestly broadened.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Optimization without built-in task-specific inductive biases leads to functional specialization in machines and potentially in brains. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The findings suggest that the functional segregation of face recognition in the brain reflects a computational optimization for visual recognition tasks. (certainty=low, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses a similar idea but narrows emphasis toward the brain interpretation and couches it more cautiously, so the earlier broader claim is clarified and somewhat narrowed in scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A convolutional neural network trained on single-cell data can infer putative regulatory motifs and their cell type-specific importance.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Scover can identify regulatory motifs and their importance from single-cell data where all parameters and outputs are easily interpretable.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same capability but adds emphasis that parameters and outputs are easily interpretable, clarifying the interpretability aspect of the model.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model developed explains 29% of the variance in gene expression across multiple mouse tissues.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model, scover, explains 29% of the variance in gene expression in multiple mouse tissues.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same numeric result and scope verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Changes in distal regulatory motifs during development can be characterized by applying scover to distal enhancers identified using scATAC-seq.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying scover to distal enhancers identified cell type-specific motif activities in distal enhancers from the developing human brain.
alignments.rationale: The published claim reports a specific application/result (developing human brain, cell type-specific motif activities) rather than the broader methodological statement in the preprint, narrowing and revising the original developmental claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: It is possible to identify regulatory motifs and their importance from single-cell data using a neural network model that is interpretable.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Scover can identify regulatory motifs and their importance from single-cell data where all parameters and outputs are easily interpretable.
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion but frames the model as easily interpretable, clarifying the interpretability claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The BigMHC method significantly improves the prediction of epitope presentation compared to four state-of-the-art classifiers.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The BigMHC method significantly improves the prediction of epitope presentation compared to four state-of-the-art classifiers.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same comparative performance claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BigMHC achieves an AUROC of 0.9733 and an AUPRC of 0.8779 on a test set of 45,409 MHC ligands.
alignments.pub_claim_text: BigMHC achieves an area under the receiver operating characteristic of 0.9733 and an area under the precision-recall curve of 0.8779.
alignments.rationale: Numeric performance metrics are reported identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: After transfer learning on immunogenicity data, BigMHC yields significantly higher precision than seven state-of-the-art models in identifying immunogenic neoepitopes.
alignments.pub_claim_text: After transfer learning on immunogenicity data, BigMHC yields significantly higher precision than seven state-of-the-art models in identifying immunogenic neoepitopes.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same post-transfer-learning precision improvement claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The computational methods presented address the challenge of identifying immunogenic neoepitopes with improved precision.
alignments.pub_claim_text: BigMHC is effective in clinical settings for identifying immunogenic neoepitopes.
alignments.rationale: The publication advances from a methodological claim about improved precision to a broader, stronger claim of clinical effectiveness, broadening scope and amplifying confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed neural network-based model incorporates Gene Ontology relationship networks to discover microbe functionality in human diseases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed neural network-based model incorporates Gene Ontology relationship networks to discover microbe functionality in human diseases.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological contribution.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model discovered and visualized novel candidates' important microbiome genes and their functions.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The discovered important microbiome genes and their functions provide novel insights into microbe functional contribution.
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the discovery as yielding novel insights rather than emphasizing visualization, clarifying the impact of the discovered candidates.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The model achieves competitive performance in predicting diseases compared to other non-Gene Ontology informed models.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model achieved competitive performance in predicting diseases compared to other non-Gene Ontology informed models.
alignments.rationale: The performance comparison is retained with equivalent framing.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The discovered candidates' important microbiome genes and their functions provide novel insights into microbe functional contribution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The discovered important microbiome genes and their functions provide novel insights into microbe functional contribution.
alignments.rationale: The publication states the same conclusion about novel insights from discovered genes and functions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep learning approach with a sparse UNET (S-UNET) achieves a median DICE score of 0.88 for automatic vascular segmentation in MSOT images.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The deep learning approach with a sparse-UNET (S-UNET) achieves a median DICE score of 0.88 for automatic vascular segmentation in MSOT images.
alignments.rationale: The reported median DICE score and task are identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The S-UNET method utilizes two wavelengths, 850 nm and 810 nm, which have physical significance for the task of vascular segmentation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The S-UNET method utilizes two wavelengths, 850 nm and 810 nm, which have physical significance for the task of vascular segmentation.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same methodological detail about wavelength selection.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed S-UNET method may enhance the clinical translation of MSOT by providing precise data-driven vascular segmentation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed approach enables precise data-driven vascular segmentation, which may enhance the clinical translation of MSOT.
alignments.rationale: The publication makes explicit that the approach enables precise segmentation and links that to potential clinical translation, clarifying the mechanism behind the tentative conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A deep-learning approach using variational autoencoder (VAE) can analyze the fluctuation and heterogeneity of chromatin structures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A deep-learning approach, variational autoencoder (VAE), can analyze the fluctuation and heterogeneity of chromatin structures revealed by single-cell imaging.
alignments.rationale: The publication clarifies that the heterogeneity/fluctuation analyzed are those revealed by single-cell imaging, adding contextual detail to the original claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The reaction coordinate identified by VAE monitors the progression of topologically associating domain (TAD) formation.
alignments.rationale: No corresponding claim about a VAE-derived reaction coordinate monitoring TAD formation appears in the publication; the specific claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Well-folded structures similar to those found in wild-type cells remain energetically favorable in cohesin-depleted cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Folding into wild-type-like structures remains energetically favorable in cohesin-depleted cells due to phase separation between chromatin segments with active and repressive histone marks.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the core result but provides an additional mechanistic explanation (phase separation), clarifying the original observation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The interaction energies in cohesin-depleted cells are not strong enough to overcome the entropic penalty, leading to partially folded structures.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The energetic stabilization in cohesin-depleted cells is not strong enough to overcome the entropic penalty, resulting in only partially folded structures and the disappearance of TADs from contact maps upon averaging.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the energetic/entropic explanation and connects it to observed disappearance of TADs, clarifying and extending the mechanistic claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The disappearance of TADs from contact maps upon averaging is a consequence of the insufficient interaction energies in cohesin-depleted cells.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The energetic stabilization in cohesin-depleted cells is not strong enough to overcome the entropic penalty, resulting in only partially folded structures and the disappearance of TADs from contact maps upon averaging.
alignments.rationale: The publication explicitly includes this consequence in the same form, linking insufficient energetic stabilization to disappearance of TADs.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Machine learning techniques, when combined with rigorous statistical mechanical analysis, are powerful tools for analyzing structural ensembles of chromatin.
alignments.rationale: The publication adds a general concluding claim about the combined utility of ML and statistical mechanics that does not appear in the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: A personalized de novo sequencing workflow was developed to identify HLA-I and HLA-II neoantigens directly from mass spectrometry data.
alignments.pub_claim_text: A personalized de novo peptide sequencing workflow can identify HLA-I and HLA-II neoantigens directly from mass spectrometry data.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same contribution with essentially identical scope and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed workflow improved the accuracy and identification rate of de novo HLA peptides by 14.3% and 38.9%, respectively.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of the workflow to datasets of five patients with melanoma expanded their predicted immunopeptidomes by 5–15%.
alignments.rationale: The publication reports a different metric and smaller percent expansion on a specific five-patient melanoma dataset, so the numeric/metric claim was revised and narrowed in scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow identified 10,440 HLA-I and 1,585 HLA-II new peptides that were not presented in existing databases.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The application of the workflow to datasets of five patients with melanoma expanded their predicted immunopeptidomes by 5–15%.
alignments.rationale: The publication omits the absolute peptide counts and instead reports percent expansion on a small melanoma cohort, so the quantitative claim was revised and narrowed.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The workflow successfully discovered 17 neoantigens of both HLA-I and HLA-II, including those with validated T cell responses.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The workflow discovered neoantigens of both HLA-I and HLA-II, including those with validated T-cell responses and those not reported in previous studies.
alignments.rationale: The publication keeps the core discovery claim but drops the explicit count (and gives slightly lower certainty), adding that some were not previously reported—so the claim is clarified and somewhat tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The deep network developed in this study is able to integrate co-abundance and compositional information without any prior knowledge.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not restate this specific methodological claim about integrating those data types without prior knowledge.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method outperforms existing state-of-the-art approaches by reconstructing 1.8 - 8 times more highly precise and complete genome bins from three different benchmark datasets.
alignments.pub_claim_text: VAMB outperforms existing state-of-the-art binners, reconstructing 29–98% and 45% more near-complete (NC) genomes on simulated and real data, respectively.
alignments.rationale: Both claims assert superior performance but the publication reports different metrics and more modest percentage gains, so the performance claim was revised and tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The application of our method to a gene catalogue of almost 10 million genes and 1,270 samples from the human gut microbiome resulted in clustering 1.3 - 1.8 million extra genes and reconstructing 117 - 246 more highly precise and complete bins.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not report these large-scale gene-catalogue application numbers or the specific gene/bin counts, so this specific applied-result claim is absent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our method identified 70 bins that were completely new compared to previous methods.
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make an explicit claim that exactly 70 entirely new bins were found compared to prior methods, so this specific claim is omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MSA Transformer captures evolutionary distance and reflects epistasis in protein sequence evolution.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MSA Transformer captures evolutionary distance and reflects epistasis in protein sequence evolution.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this result claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Systematic shuffling of natural and simulated MSAs demonstrates that the model exploits column-wise conservation to distinguish phylogenetic relationships.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Systematic shuffling of natural and simulated MSAs demonstrates that the model exploits column-wise conservation to distinguish phylogenetic relationships.
alignments.rationale: The publication presents the same experiment and conclusion.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using internal embeddings, MSA Transformer reconstructs trees that are markedly consistent with trees generated by maximum likelihood inference.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Using internal embeddings, we reconstruct trees that are markedly consistent with those generated by maximum likelihood inference.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates this finding with equivalent wording and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Applying MSA Transformer to RNA-dependent RNA polymerase of RNA viruses and the nucleo-cytoplasmic large DNA virus domain recovers both established and novel evolutionary relationships.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Applying this approach to both the RNA-dependent RNA polymerase of RNA viruses and the nucleo-cytoplasmic large DNA virus domain, we recover both established and novel evolutionary relationships.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this application and its outcomes with the same scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: MSA Transformer complements, rather than replaces, classical inference for more accurate histories of protein families.
alignments.pub_claim_text: MSA Transformer complements, rather than replaces, classical inference for more accurate histories of protein families.
alignments.rationale: Both versions state the same conclusion about complementarity with classical methods.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We find four types of epistasis (Synergistic, Sign, Antagonistic, Saturation), which can be both short or long-range and have a simple mechanical interpretation.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The study identifies four types of epistasis (Synergistic, Sign, Antagonistic, Saturation) that can be both short or long-range and have a simple mechanical interpretation.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this classification and interpretation directly.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) predicts well mutation costs but is a rather poor generative model.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Direct Coupling Analysis (DCA) predicts well the cost of point mutations but is a poor generative model.
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the same assessment of DCA's predictive vs generative performance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: DCA can predict short-range epistasis but fails to capture long-range epistasis, in agreement with empirical findings.
alignments.pub_claim_text: DCA can predict short-range epistasis but fails to capture long-range epistasis, consistent with empirical findings.
alignments.rationale: The publication conveys the same result and agreement with empirical data.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The failure of DCA to capture long-range epistasis is generic when function requires subparts to work in concert.
alignments.pub_claim_text: The failure of DCA to capture long-range epistasis is proposed to be generic when function requires subparts to work in concert.
alignments.rationale: Both texts convey the same proposed generality, though the publication frames it as a proposal; the meaning remains aligned.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Neural networks may be a more promising tool to measure epistasis compared to DCA.
alignments.pub_claim_text: Other methods may be better suited to capture long-range effects in allosteric proteins.
alignments.rationale: The publication broadens the suggestion to 'other methods' rather than naming neural networks specifically, and is more cautious about which methods are preferable.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PredIDR provides accurate prediction of intrinsically disordered regions in proteins.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PredIDR provides accurate prediction of intrinsically disordered regions in proteins.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats this performance claim verbatim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PredIDR was as accurate as the top state-of-the-art IDR prediction methods in the second round of CAID.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PredIDR was as accurate as the top state-of-the-art IDR prediction methods in the second round of CAID.
alignments.rationale: The publication retains the same benchmarking claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: PredIDR was trained using missing residues in the Protein Data Bank as positive examples.
alignments.pub_claim_text: PredIDR was trained using missing residues in the Protein Data Bank as positive examples.
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the same methodological training detail.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The design of a sigma factor-based perceptron as a molecular classifier is demonstrated to work on the principles of molecular sequestration. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The design of a sigma factor-based perceptron as a molecular classifier is demonstrated to function based on molecular sequestration principles. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Wording differs only slightly but the claim content and certainty are the same: the perceptron functions via molecular sequestration.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The output of the biomolecular perceptron is affected by the competitive binding of sigma factors to a pool of shared and limited resources of core RNA polymerase. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The output of the biomolecular perceptron is affected by the competitive binding of sigma factors to a pool of shared and limited resources of core RNA polymerase. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text and meaning are effectively identical between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Sharing limited resources influences multi-layer perceptron neural networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The influence of sharing limited resources on multilayer perceptron neural networks is revealed. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both state the same conclusion that resource sharing influences multilayer perceptrons; only phrasing and label differ.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Design principles are outlined that enable the construction of non-linear classifiers using sigma-based biomolecular neural networks in the presence of competitive resource-sharing effects. (certainty=medium, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Design principles are outlined that enable the construction of nonlinear classifiers using sigma-based biomolecular neural networks in the presence of competitive resource-sharing effects. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Same design-principles claim; only minor formatting and type-label change (conclusion/result) occurred.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shRNAI model trained with gRNA sequences alone predicted more potent shRNAmir gRNAs than the existing algorithms. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: shRNAI, a convolutional neural network model, predicts highly potent shRNAmir gRNAs and outperforms previous algorithms even when trained solely on gRNA sequences. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication restates the same performance claim with the same emphasis that training on gRNA sequences alone yields superior predictions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shRNAI+ model, trained with additional features of shRNAmir processibility and target site context, showed further improvement on both public and experimental test datasets. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The model shRNAI+ improves performance by incorporating features related to shRNAmir processability and target site context. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same claim that shRNAI+ improves performance by adding processability and context features; publication paraphrases but maintains claim and certainty.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The shRNAI models exhibited better performance for the CNNC motif despite being trained on datasets generated with the CNNC motif-free shRNAmir backbone. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: shRNAI demonstrates improved performance on the CNNC motif despite being initially trained on datasets built with a CNNC motif-free pri-miR-30 backbone. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication adds a specific backbone name (pri-miR-30) but preserves the core result about CNNC performance; this is a clarification rather than substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study provides a rational framework for designing shRNAmir gRNAs for targets. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides a robust framework for designing potent shRNAmir gRNAs and a versatile tool for developing RNAi therapeutics. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication keeps the framework claim but expands its framing to emphasize potency and therapeutic utility, broadening the claimed applications.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The study offers a means of designing optimal RNA interference drugs necessary for RNA therapeutics. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This study provides a robust framework for designing potent shRNAmir gRNAs and a versatile tool for developing RNAi therapeutics. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the contribution as a 'versatile tool' for RNAi therapeutics rather than explicitly guaranteeing optimal RNAi drugs; the scope is broadened but the certainty/strength of the drug-design claim is somewhat tempered in wording.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Grid cells perform path integration over short trajectories and change their reference frames within single trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Grid cells track the animal movement in multiple reference frames within single trials. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the within-trial multiple reference-frame result but omits the explicit 'path integration over short trajectories' phrasing from the preprint, narrowing the stated scope of the claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Grid cell modules re-anchor to a task-relevant object via a translation of the grid pattern. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Grid cells estimate position within multiple local reference frames rather than operating as a global positioning system. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication's conclusion that grid cells operate within local reference frames is consistent with the preprint's re-anchoring-to-local-object observation; the core idea is preserved.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The code for movement direction in grid modules drifts as the animal navigates using self-motion cues. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The internal representation of movement direction in grid cells drifted during self-motion navigation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Verbatim conceptual match: both state that the movement-direction code drifts during self-motion navigation.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This drift predicts the homing direction of the mouse. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: This drift predicted the mouse’s homing direction. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The claim linking drift to homing direction is preserved, but the publication reports it with reduced certainty (high -> medium), so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The classification accuracy rate reached 95.24% for 120 breeds and 99.34% for 76 selected breeds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The classification accuracy rate reached 95.24% for 120 breeds and 99.34% for 76 selected breeds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Exact numeric result is reproduced identically in the publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: This study demonstrates a significant improvement over existing methods using the same Stanford Dog Dataset. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The proposed method demonstrates a significant improvement over existing methods using the same Stanford Dog Dataset. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication reiterates the same improvement-over-existing-methods conclusion using the same dataset.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The highest accuracy rate for dog breed classification is still below expectations compared to other animals or plants. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The diversity of dog breeds continues to pose a challenge to classification accuracy. (certainty=medium, type=limitation)
alignments.rationale: The preprint's broader 'below expectations' phrasing is reframed in the publication as a specific limitation (breed diversity); the focus of the limitation is revised though both are limitations.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The proposed method is expected to serve as a fundamental framework for accurate classification of a wider range of species. (certainty=low, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication does not make or repeat the forward-looking claim about serving as a fundamental framework for broader species classification, so this preprint claim appears omitted.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The dimensionality of RNN activity depends on the task parameters and evolves over time and over stages of learning. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Gradient descent can lead recurrent neural networks (RNNs) to compress the dimensionality of their representations in a way that matches task demands during training. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication links the observation about dimensionality and task demands to the learning mechanism (gradient descent), adding explanatory detail while preserving the core result about task-dependent dimensionality.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Common solutions produced by the network naturally compress dimensionality, while variability-inducing chaos can expand it. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: RNNs can require an expansion of dimensionality in early timesteps and compression in later ones. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Both describe compression and expansion dynamics; the publication frames this as a temporal pattern (early expansion then compression), clarifying the dynamics implied by the preprint.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Chaotic networks balance the factors of dimensionality compression and expansion to solve the discrimination task with high accuracy and good generalization properties. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Strongly chaotic RNNs appear particularly adept at learning the balance between dimensionality expansion and compression. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication preserves the claim that chaotic networks balance expansion/compression, but reports it with somewhat lower expressed certainty, so confidence is tempered.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: These findings shed light on mechanisms by which artificial neural networks solve tasks while forming compact representations that may generalize well. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Simple gradient-based learning rules lead neural networks to solve tasks with robust representations that generalize to new cases. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Publication retains the same conclusion about compact, generalizable representations and presents it more strongly (higher certainty), effectively amplifying the confidence in the finding.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Geometric shapes induce a hypoactivation of ventral visual areas and an overactivation of the intraparietal and inferior temporal regions during perception. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Geometric shapes induce a hypoactivation of ventral visual areas and an overactivation of the intraparietal and inferior temporal regions during perception. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The preprint and publication claims are verbatim and carry the same certainty and claim type.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The activation of brain regions involved in mathematical processing is modulated by geometric regularity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The activation of brain regions involved in mathematical processing is modulated by geometric regularity. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The statement is identical between preprint and publication with no substantive change.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Convolutional neural networks fail to account for subsequent dorsal parietal and prefrontal signals related to geometric shapes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Convolutional neural networks fail to account for subsequent dorsal parietal and prefrontal signals related to geometric shapes. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: Claim text and meaning are the same in both versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The perception of abstract geometric regularities engages an additional symbolic mode of visual perception. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The perception of abstract geometric regularities engages an additional symbolic mode of visual perception. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: No difference in wording or emphasis between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The image features extracted from HCC histopathological images using the pre-trained CNN models can accurately distinguish normal and cancer samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Image features extracted from HCC histopathological images using the pre-trained CNN models VGG 16, Inception V3 and ResNet 50 can accurately distinguish normal and cancer samples. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication specifies which pre-trained CNNs were used, clarifying the original general statement without changing its scope or confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: 21.4% and 16% of image features on average were significantly associated with overall survival and disease-free survival, respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: In a univariate Cox regression analysis, 21.4% and 16% of image features on average were significantly associated with overall survival (OS) and disease-free survival (DFS), respectively. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds the analytical context (univariate Cox regression) but preserves the numeric associations, so the claim is clarified.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The optimal two subgroups discovered using Inception image features were significantly associated with both overall and disease-free survival. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The optimal two subgroups discovered using Inception model image features showed significant differences in both overall (C-index = 0.628 and p = 7.39E-07) and DFS (C-index = 0.558 and p = 0.012). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Publication provides quantitative performance metrics (C-index and p-values), clarifying and supporting the preprint subgroup survival claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: There are significant correlations between the extracted image features and integrated biological pathways derived from gene expression and copy number variation. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our work demonstrates the utility of extracting image features using pre-trained models by using them to build accurate prognostic models of HCC as well as highlight significant correlations between these features, clinical survival, and relevant biological pathways. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames the correlations within a broader conclusion (utility for prognostic models) and expresses the finding with higher overall emphasis, broadening scope and amplifying confidence.

alignments.pub_claim_text: Using an elastic net regularized Cox Proportional Hazards model of OS constructed from Inception image features, we obtained a concordance index (C-index) of 0.789 and a significant log-rank test (p = 7.6E-18). (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: This detailed prognostic-model result (elastic net model performance) does not appear in the preprint and is an added publication claim.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Genesis is capable of reconstructing native-like distance-and angle distributions for five native folds and three novel, so-called 'dark-matter' folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genesis is capable of reconstructing native-like distance and angle distributions for five native folds and three novel 'dark-matter' folds. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication repeats the result with effectively identical wording and confidence.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The Genesis framework enables the exploration of the protein sequence and fold space within minutes and is not bound to specific protein topologies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Genesis enables exploration of the protein fold space within minutes, unrestricted by protein topologies. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.rationale: The publication restates the contribution more concisely (drop of explicit "sequence"), clarifying the claim while preserving meaning and scope.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Our approach addresses the backbone designability problem, showing that structural patterns in proteins can be efficiently learned by small neural networks. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: Our approach addresses the backbone designability problem, showing that small neural networks can efficiently learn structural patterns in proteins. (certainty=high, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication expresses the same conclusion with higher stated certainty, indicating amplification of confidence while keeping content consistent.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: We obtained encouraging success rates for folded proteins and further biochemically characterized folded designs. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: We obtained encouraging success rates for folded proteins through a high-throughput assay that characterized the stability of the designs via protease resistance. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication adds methodological detail (high-throughput protease-resistance assay) clarifying how success rates were measured, without changing the claim's substance.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: The mean growth rate of Staphylococcus aureus in control cultures was estimated to be 60.3 μm/h for the first 24 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: The mean growth rate of Staphylococcus aureus in control cultures was estimated to be 60.3 μm/h for the first 24 hours. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Identical numeric result and wording between preprint and publication.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: Using a mixed effect model, the estimated growth rates were 51.0 μm/h for control cultures and 36.5 μm/h for rifampicin pretreated cultures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: A mixed effect model estimated a lower mean growth rate of 51.0 μm/h for control cultures and 36.5 μm/h for rifampicin pretreated cultures. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Same estimated values and model; wording differences are minor and do not change meaning.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: An increase in the number of neighboring colonies reduces the colony growth rate in the control group but has a less typical effect in the rifampicin-pretreated group. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.pub_claim_text: An increase in the number of neighboring colonies reduces the colony growth rate in the control group but has a less typical effect in the rifampicin-pretreated group. (certainty=medium, type=result)
alignments.rationale: Text and meaning are the same across versions.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: CNN-based bacterial colony detection and analysis of growth dynamics may serve as an accurate and efficient tool for bacteriological work and research. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.pub_claim_text: CNN-based bacterial colony detection and analysis of growth dynamics may serve as an accurate and efficient tool for bacteriological research. (certainty=medium, type=conclusion)
alignments.rationale: The publication slightly tightens wording (drops "work and") but the conclusion is essentially the same; this is a minor clarification.

alignments.preprint_claim_text: BatchDTA alleviates the impact of batch effects on DNN models used for drug-target affinity estimation. (certainty=high, type=contribution)
alignments.pub_claim_text: BatchDTA can successfully learn the ranking orders of the compounds from multiple batches. (certainty=high, type=result)
alignments.rationale: The publication frames a specific mechanism/evidence (learning ranking orders across batches) that supports the preprint's higher-level claim about alleviating batch effects; this is a clarification rather than contradiction.
